full_response:
Below we work in units with c=1 for simplicity (you can reinsert c by dimensional analysis at the end).

1.  Start from the Schwarzschild “t–r” line‐element  
   ds²=−(1−2GM/r) dt²+ (1−2GM/r)^{−1}dr²+ r²dΩ².  

2.  Introduce the tortoise coordinate  
   r*≡r+2GM ln∣r/(2GM)−1∣,  
   so that  
   dr* = dr/(1−2GM/r).  

3.  Define the ingoing/outgoing null coordinates  
   u≡t−r*,    v≡t+r*.  
   Then  
   du=dt−dr/(1−2GM/r),   dv=dt+dr/(1−2GM/r),  
   and one shows  
   −(1−2GM/r) dt²+ (1−2GM/r)^{−1}dr² = −(1−2GM/r)\,du\,dv.  

4.  Now define Kruskal–Szekeres null coordinates  
   U=− e^{−u/(4GM)},    V= e^{ v/(4GM)}.  

   ⇒  
   dU = −(1/(4GM)) e^{−u/(4GM)} du = −(U/(4GM)) du  
   ⇒ du = −(4GM/U)\,dU,  

   dV = (1/(4GM)) e^{v/(4GM)} dv = (V/(4GM)) dv  
   ⇒ dv = (4GM/V)\,dV.  

   Hence  
   du dv = [−(4GM/U)dU]⋅[(4GM/V)dV]  
         = −(16 G²M²)/(UV)\;dU dV.  

5.  Substitute into the null‐form of the metric:  
   ds²_{t−r} = −(1−2GM/r)\;du dv  
             = −(1−2GM/r)⋅[−16 G²M²/(UV)]\,dU dV  
             = +16 G²M²⋅(1−2GM/r)/(UV)\;dU dV.  

6.  Use the implicit relation (from U V definition)  
   U V = −exp[(v−u)/(4GM)] = −exp[r*/(2GM)]  
        = −(r/(2GM)−1)\,e^{r/(2GM)}.  

   One finds  
   (1−2GM/r)/(U V) = −(2GM/r)\,e^{−r/(2GM)}.  

   Therefore  
   ds²_{t−r} = 16 G²M² ⋅ [−(2GM/r)e^{−r/(2GM)}] dU dV  
             = −(32 G³M³/r)\,e^{−r/(2GM)}\,dU dV.  

7.  Finally add back the angular part unchanged.  

Boxing the result:  
\boxed{  
ds² = -\,\frac{32\,G^3\,M^3}{r}\,\exp\!\biggl(-\frac{r}{2\,G\,M}\biggr)\,dU\,dV \;+\;r^2\,d\Omega^2,  
}  

where r=r(UV) is defined implicitly by   
U\,V = -\Bigl(\frac r{2GM}-1\Bigr)\,e^{\,r/(2GM)}.  

Confidence: 97%
